nativeRange.habitat: The Estuary Seahorse inhabits shallow coastal waters across a depth range of 0 to 68 meters, though most individuals are found above 8 meters. It frequents seagrass beds, estuaries, lagoons, harbors, mangroves, and the shallower edges of reefs, and is tolerant of both marine and brackish water conditions.

diet.notes: The Estuary Seahorse requires live or frozen mysid shrimp, enriched brine shrimp (artemia), amphipods, and small crustaceans, offered in four to five small portions spread throughout the day. Fry need live zooplankton such as copepods and rotifers to survive; adults can generally be acclimated to accept frozen mysis shrimp as their staple diet. Because seahorses are slow, deliberate feeders, they tend to lose out to faster-moving tankmates at feeding time — one of the main reasons a species-only or similarly slow-feeding setup is so strongly recommended.

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer: Emőke Dénes. Photographed at Sea Life Centre Hannover, Germany, December 2019. File dimensions: 2595 × 3459 px. Yellow morph individual clearly identified as Hippocampus kuda.
Photographer: J. Greenfield; location: Indonesia. FishBase species gallery image. License not explicitly stated on page.
Product photo from LiveAquaria Estuary Seahorse Tank Bred listing. Clean, high-resolution retailer image. Identified as H. cf kuda (Sri Lanka captive-bred stock).

summary: The Estuary Seahorse (Hippocampus kuda) is a slow-moving, peaceful species from Indo-Pacific seagrass beds and estuaries. It requires a dedicated species tank with minimal water flow, hitching posts to anchor itself, and multiple small feedings of live or frozen mysid shrimp each day.
careNotes:
The Estuary Seahorse does best in a species-specific aquarium or one shared only with pipefish — any faster-moving fish will outcompete it at feeding time, leading to starvation. A minimum tank size of 50 gallons (189 L) is recommended for a pair, with an additional 10 to 15 gallons for each further pair. Water flow should be kept low to moderate, as strong currents stress seahorses and make it harder for them to cling to surfaces. Branching gorgonians, artificial coral, macroalgae, or similar structures serve as hitching posts, giving the seahorse a place to anchor itself — these are not optional décor but a genuine behavioral need.

Feeding should happen four to five times per day. Frozen mysis shrimp and enriched brine shrimp (artemia) form the core diet; live copepods and amphipods encourage natural foraging behavior. Stable water quality is essential: temperature 22–27°C (72–78°F), avoiding anything above 27°C (80°F) as warmer water raises susceptibility to bacterial Vibrio infections. Target a pH of 8.1–8.4, a specific gravity of 1.021–1.025 (equivalent to 26–33 ppt), and a carbonate hardness (dKH) of 8 to 12.

Hippocampus kuda holds Vulnerable status on the IUCN Red List and is listed under CITES Appendix II, meaning international trade is regulated. Tank-bred specimens are strongly preferred over wild-caught individuals. Some captive-bred stock sold in the trade originates from Sri Lanka and may be labeled as H. cf. kuda, reflecting ongoing taxonomic review of that population.
breedingNotes: The Estuary Seahorse is ovoviviparous — the female deposits eggs into the male's ventral brood pouch (a specialized pocket on his abdomen), where he fertilizes and carries them until birth. Gestation typically lasts 20 to 28 days, with broods ranging from around 20 to 1,000 fry depending on the male's size and overall health. Despite the seahorse's reputation for lifelong monogamy, this bond has not been reliably confirmed in laboratory conditions. Breeding in captivity is achievable but demanding: newborn fry require live zooplankton such as rotifers and copepods, fed frequently throughout the day, and survival rates depend heavily on maintaining a constant supply of appropriately sized live food.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Estuary Seahorse Tank Bred (Sri Lanka) product page. Primary source for care parameters, tank requirements, and diet. Note: parent URL in manifest was the seahorses collection page; actual species page used.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 30 cm TL confirmed), temperature range (21.3–29°C), native range, taxonomy (Syngnathidae / Syngnathiformes), and diet (zooplankton/carnivore). Minor disagreement on temperature ceiling: FishBase max 29°C vs LiveAquaria recommended max ~25.6°C (78°F); conservative 27°C used.

additional.name: LiveAquaria (Kuda Seahorse, Captive-Bred)
additional.notes: Second LiveAquaria listing for same species (H. kuda captive-bred). Confirmed: care level Difficult, peaceful temperament, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, reef compatible. This listing gives 30-gal minimum vs 50-gal on the Estuary page; conservative 50-gal used.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Hippocampus kuda)
additional.notes: Confirmed size range (17–30 cm), habitat description (estuaries, lagoons, seagrass beds), temperature (72–77°F / 22–25°C), pH (8.1–8.4), salinity tolerance (18–36 ppt). Used for range and habitat cross-verification.

fish.breedingNotes: The male carries eggs in his ventral brood pouch for 20 to 28 days, producing broods of 20 to 1,000 fry. Newborn fry require live zooplankton and frequent feedings. Captive breeding is achievable in dedicated species tanks but is generally considered demanding.
